planting.note: Plant in full to partial sun in moist to wet, acidic soil; camu camu is native to seasonally flooded Amazonian riverbanks and tolerates waterlogging far better than most fruit plants — it can actually grow with its roots in standing water for months. Zone 10-12 only in the US.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; camu camu (Myrciaria dubia) is a shrubby Amazonian Myrtaceae producing small, cherry-like, reddish-purple fruits with a powerfully acidic, cranberry-passion-fruit flavor that are virtually inedible raw due to their extreme sourness but are valued as one of the highest natural sources of vitamin C on earth — 20–60 times more vitamin C per 100g than oranges. The fruit is processed into powders, juices, and health supplements widely sold in health food stores; home production is possible in south Florida and Hawaii with specimens growing in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil or at pond edges. An outstanding addition to a food-forest understory in zone 10+, where it also provides white, fragrant flowers and attractive foliage.

planting.note: Plant seedlings or air-layered cuttings in deep, moist, fertile, well-drained tropical soil; marang requires genuine tropical conditions (zone 11-12) and high humidity; it is cultivated in southern Mindanao (Philippines), Borneo, and throughout Southeast Asia as a valued fruit tree.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, rich, consistently moist, well-drained tropical soil; marang (Artocarpus odoratissimus) is a close relative of breadfruit and jackfruit, native to Borneo and Mindanao, producing rounded, yellow-green fruits about the size of a small jackfruit with an extremely sweet, creamy white flesh surrounding large, edible seeds — many who have tasted it rank it among the finest tropical fruits, with a rich, sweet, almost vanilla-custard flavor with a fragrant aroma. The seeds are also edible when cooked (roasted, boiled, or ground) and have a pleasant chestnut-like flavor. Strictly tropical and not tolerant of any frost; best suited for south Florida and Hawaii collections and food-forest enthusiasts in zone 11+. Less well-known outside Southeast Asia but increasingly available from specialty nurseries.

planting.note: Plant in deep, moist, fertile, well-drained tropical soil in full sun; pulasan requires warm tropical conditions with high humidity and no frost; it is strictly a zone 11-12 tree in the United States, found in south Florida and Hawaii botanical gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, fertile, moist, well-drained tropical soil; pulasan (Nephelium ramboutan-ake) is a close relative of rambutan (Nephelium lappaceum) native to the Malaysian peninsula, producing clusters of oval, red fruits with short, thick, blunt protuberances (versus rambutan's long, flexible hairs) — distinguishing it from rambutan at a glance. The white, translucent, sweet, juicy flesh is widely considered sweeter and better-flavored than rambutan, and the seed of pulasan is also edible (unlike rambutan's bitter seed) — roasted or boiled with a flavor similar to cocoa or almond. A premium tropical fruit rarely available commercially outside Southeast Asia; highly prized by tropical fruit collectors. Requires high humidity, consistent moisture, and no frost.

common_name: Guabiyú (Uruguayan Guabiju, Black Cherry of the Forest)

planting.note: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; guabiyú is a subtropical Myrtaceae from southern Brazil, Uruguay, and Argentina, tolerating light frosts and making it suitable for zone 9 in sheltered locations — one of the hardier South American Myrtaceae for Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; guabiyú (Myrcianthes pungens) is a subtropical rainforest tree of southern South America producing small, round, deep purple to black berries in clusters, with a sweet, aromatic, cherry-like flavor and a pleasant Myrtaceae fragrance — consumed fresh, in jams, juices, and liqueurs throughout its native range. The tree is more cold-hardy than most Myrtaceae relatives, tolerating temperatures down to 25°F (-4°C) when established, making it potentially the most cold-tolerant South American Myrtaceae and an excellent candidate for zone 9 Gulf Coast fruit gardens. The attractive, reddish-barked tree also provides ornamental value year-round with glossy leaves. A growing interest among Gulf Coast gardeners and subtropical fruit collectors.

planting.note: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; uvaia prefers subtropical to tropical conditions and can tolerate brief light frosts once established — suited to zone 9b-12 in the US.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; uvaia (Eugenia pyriformis) is a beautiful Brazilian Myrtaceae tree closely related to Surinam cherry and pitanga, producing pear-shaped to round yellow fruits about 1 inch in diameter with a juicy, aromatic, sweet-acidic flesh — the flavor is often described as a complex blend of passion fruit, citrus, and Eugenia aromatics with a pleasant acidity that makes the fruit excellent fresh, as juice, in ice cream, and as jams. The tree has attractive bronze-red new growth and small white flowers. Increasingly popular among Brazilian native fruit enthusiasts and tropical fruit collectors in south Florida and Hawaii; one of the finest of the genus Eugenia for flavor. Fruits appear in clusters along the branches and trunk.
